key_insight: A minimizing-movement discretization can separate normal geometric evolution from tangential mesh motion, while using admissible constraints to preserve stability and recover classical curvature-flow schemes.
engineering_relevance: This gives a robust finite-element framework for simulating curvature-driven interfaces with unconditional energy stability and controllable mesh quality, which is useful in solid mechanics and surface-evolution problems.
method: Use a time-stepping variational problem with a dissipation term for normal displacement plus surface Dirichlet energy.
Choose the geometric flow via the metric: L2 for mean curvature flow and H-1 for surface diffusion flow.
Select tangential motion independently through weak deformation constraints; admissibility requires the identity map to satisfy the constraint.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports fully discrete schemes with existence and uniqueness under nondegeneracy assumptions, unconditional energy stability, and numerical experiments comparing admissible versus classical schemes.
watch_item: Performance depends on admissibility and nondegeneracy conditions, so constraint design and mesh regularity must be handled carefully in implementation.
paper_title: A minimizing-movement framework for geometric gradient flows with admissible tangential motion
